title: William Hopoate

Career Stats & Summary|publisher=Rugby League Project|access-date=2015-12-07}} |updated = 5 November 2023 | module = {{infobox person | embed = yes | father = [[John Hopoate]] | mother = | spouse = | education = | alma_mater = | relatives = [[Albert Hopoate (rugby league, born 2001)|Albert Hopoate]] (brother)
[[Jamil Hopoate]] (brother)
[[Lehi Hopoate]] (brother)
[[Albert Hopoate (rugby league, born 1985)|Albert Hopoate]] (uncle) }} }} '''William Hopoate''' ({{langx|to|Viliami Hopoate}}; born 9 May 1992) also known by the [[nickname]] of '''"Hoppa"''',{{cite news|last=Lane|first=Daniel|title=Battle of Will: clubs all want a piece of Hoppa, says agent|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/league-news/battle-of-will-clubs-all-want-a-piece-of-hoppa-says-agent-20110621-1gdmb.html|access-date=25 June 2011|newspaper=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|date=22 June 2011}} is a former [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tonga]] international [[rugby league]] footballer. He played for the [[Manly Warringah Sea Eagles]], [[Parramatta Eels]] and [[Canterbury Bankstown Bulldogs]] in the [[National Rugby League]]. Hopoate has also played for [[City New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales City]] and [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] in the [[State of Origin series]]. ==Early life== Hopoate was born in [[Manly, New South Wales]], Australia. He is of [[Tonga]]n descent He attended [[Cromer Campus]] and represented [[Australian Schoolboys rugby league team]].{{cite web |url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/hoppa-jr-signs-300k-contract/news-story/68559e0bd6491796d6a6dcb111b92635 |title=Hoppa Jr signs $300k contract |date=18 June 2008 |publisher=Fox League |access-date=14 October 2021}} Hopoate is the son of former [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian international]], [[John Hopoate]]. ==Club career== ===Manly Warringah Sea Eagles=== He joined Manly's squads in 2009 at the age of 16 and played the entire year in the Sea Eagles [[National Youth Competition (rugby league)|Toyota Cup]]. He primarily played at {{rlp|fb}} but also played on the [[Rugby league positions#wings|wing]] and in the [[Rugby league positions#centres|centres]]. Despite a season-ending injury to Manly 1st-grade fullback [[Brett Stewart (rugby league)|Brett Stewart]] in Round 1 of the [[2010 NRL season]], Hopoate had to wait until Round 13 to make his debut as coach [[Des Hasler]] preferred to use [[Ben Farrar]] in the custodian role. Hopoate is the son of former Manly player [[John Hopoate]], though other than his physical size, speed, strength and general play, he has shown none of the "bad boy" habits which saw his father often on the sidelines through suspension. Hopoate also represented the [[Australia Schoolboy rugby league team|Australian Schoolboys team]].{{cite web |url=https://amp.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/schoolboys-take-it-to-the-world/news-story/56ebf9ce74d1920402aeb534a4cd0c1a |title=Schoolboys take it to the world |author=James Phelps |publisher=Herald Sun |date=23 August 2008 |access-date=14 October 2021}} [[File:Will Hopoate.jpg|thumb|right|Hopoate playing for the Manly Sea Eagles against the [[Sydney Roosters]] in 2011]] In Round 3 of the 2011 NRL season, Hopoate scored his first ever double against the [[Newcastle Knights]] at [[Brookvale Oval]]. Hopoate was part of the Manly side that defeated the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in the [[2011 NRL Grand Final]]{{cite web|url=https://www.mormon.org.uk/william|title=Hi I'm William Hopoate|publisher=Mormon.org.uk|access-date=8 May 2017}} where he was denied the opening try but did produce a flick pass that sent [[Glenn Stewart]] over the line for a crucial try in the second half. ===Parramatta Eels=== In October 2011, Hopoate signed a two-year contract with the [[Parramatta Eels]] starting in 2014, after his Mormon mission ended.{{cite web|url=http://www.zerotackle.com/nrl/william-hopoate-sign-parramatta-eels-4214/#PkyAkq3g4TD76bkU.97|title=Will Hopoate signs with Parramatta Eels | NRL|publisher=Zero Tackle|date=2011-10-04|access-date=2015-12-07}} In April 2015, he agreed to re-sign with the Parramatta club on a three-year contract,{{cite web|url=http://www.zerotackle.com/nrl/hopoate-set-to-extend-stay-with-parramatta-17133/#D6lpSDbOCP61x9mA.97|title=Hopoate set to extend stay with Parramatta | NRL|publisher=Zero Tackle|date=2015-04-18|access-date=2015-12-07}} however due to legal complications, the contract wasn't registered and Hopoate was left a free agent.{{cite web|url=http://www.zerotackle.com/nrl/parramatta-offer-hopoate-unders-to-stay-19384/#s2m6LkBtFggx7OPd.97|title=Parramatta offer Hopoate 'unders' to stay | NRL|publisher=Zero Tackle|access-date=2015-12-07}} In 2016, Hopoate took the Parramatta Eels to court and was seeking $1.83 million in damages.{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/eels/will-hopoates-battle-with-parramatta-moves-from-courtroom-to-the-field/news-story/abbc32b59c8352b4ee45c930e1490c25/|title=Will Hopoate’s battle with Parramatta moves from courtroom to the field|website=www.foxsports.com.au}} On 29 October 2016, both parties came to an agreement of a $400,000 settlement.{{cite web|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/canterbury-bulldogs/canterbury-bulldogs-star-will-hopoate-seeking-183-million-from-parramatta-eels-20160331-gnvcg2.html|title=Canterbury Bulldogs star Will Hopoate seeking $1.83 million from Parramatta Eels|first1=Adrian|last1=Proszenko|first2=Louise|last2=Hall|date=31 March 2016|via=The Sydney Morning Herald}}{{cite news|url=https://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/nrl/teams/eels/will-hopoates-battle-with-parramatta-moves-from-courtroom-to-the-field/news-story/abbc32b59c8352b4ee45c930e1490c25|title=Hopoate's Parramatta battle moves to the field|newspaper=Daily Telegraph|date=14 March 2016|last1=Nicolussi|first1=Christian}} ===Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs=== On 7 December 2015, Hopoate signed a 2-year contract with the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] starting in 2016.{{cite web|url=http://www.zerotackle.com/nrl/bulldogs-sign-will-hopoate-19881/#vcx38txCWpZLBTr1.97|title=Bulldogs sign Will Hopoate | NRL|publisher=Zero Tackle|date=2015-05-27|access-date=2015-12-07}} In his first season with Canterbury, Hopoate announced that he would not be playing for the club on Sundays due to his religious faith. Hopoate even went as far to say that he would not play for Canterbury in the grand final if the club was to make it that far as the final is on a Sunday. Hopoate missed Canterbury's qualifying final against Penrith due to the game being played on Sunday.{{cite web|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/canterbury-bulldogs/will-hopoates-absence-for-the-canterbury-bulldogs-on-sundays-isnt-about-religion--its-about-football-20160905-gr93hz.html|title=Will Hopoate's absence for the Canterbury Bulldogs on Sundays isn't about religion – it's about football|first=Andrew|last=Webster|date=5 September 2016|via=The Sydney Morning Herald}}{{cite news|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2016-03-30/will-hopoate-to-choose-god-over-nrl-grand-final/7283432|title=Hopoate set to choose God over grand final|newspaper=ABC News|date=30 March 2016}} On 14 May 2017, Hopoate reversed his decision and declared that he would be playing Sunday games again for Canterbury.{{cite news|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2017-05-15/will-hopoate-to-play-sundays-in-nrl-for-canterbury-bulldogs/8527796|title=Will Hopoate agrees to play Sundays for Bulldogs|newspaper=ABC News|date=15 May 2017}} On 1 June 2017, Hopoate re-signed with the Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs for a further three years until 2020.{{citation needed|date=June 2023}} On 27 November 2019, Hopoate signed a two-year contract extension to remain at Canterbury until the end of the 2021 season.{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/transfer-centre-bulldogs-make-seventh-resigning-storm-gun-locked-in/news-story/2c73e7014e7d36840b2eb093a76d8d57/|title=Bulldogs make seventh re-signing, Storm gun locked in|website=Fox Sports|date=29 November 2019}} Hopoate made a total of 13 appearances for Canterbury in the [[2020 NRL season]]. The club finished in 15th place on the table, only avoiding the [[Wooden Spoon (award)|Wooden Spoon]] by for and against.{{cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/teams/bulldogs/nrl-2020-canterburybankstown-bulldogs-announce-eight-player-departures-kieran-foran-aiden-tolman-full-list/news-story/67fbadc55112e80ee342e26861bd206d|title=Dogs confirm EIGHT-man clean-out in first glimpse of Barrett era|website=www.foxsports.com.au|date=22 September 2020}} On 10 August 2021, Hopoate signed a two-year deal with English side [[St Helens RFC]] with the option of a third season.{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/12377813/will-hopoate-st-helens-clinch-signing-of-tonga-world-cup-captain-for-2022-super-league-season/|title=St Helens clinch signing of Tonga World Cup captain for 2022 Super League season|website=www.skysports.com}} Hopoate made a total of 24 appearances for Canterbury in the [[2021 NRL season]] as the club finished last and claimed the Wooden Spoon.{{cite web|url=https://wwos.nine.com.au/nrl/canterbury-bulldogs-set-to-sign-max-king-and-braidon-burns/a9fa54c9-a224-4cc9-ae15-8a5dd1e74275/|title=Canterbury Bulldogs set to sign Rabbitohs star Braidon Burns, Storm prop Max King for 2022 NRL season|website=wwos.nine.com.au}} ===St Helens=== [[File:Viliami Hopoate.jpg|thumb|Hopoate catching the ball for Tonga at the 2021 RLWC]] In round 1 of the 2022 Super League season, Hopoate made his club debut for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] where they defeated the [[Catalans Dragons]] 28-8.{{cite web|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/st-helens-beat-catalans-in-fiery-season-opener-talking-points/|title=St Helens beat Catalans in fiery season opener – talking points|website=www.loverugbyleague.com|date=10 February 2022}} On 24 September 2022, Hopoate played at centre in St Helens 24-12 Super League Grand Final victory over [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]].{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/sep/24/st-helens-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report-rugby-league/|title=St Helens sink Leeds to win record fourth consecutive Grand Final|website=www.theguardian.com}} On 18 February 2023, Hopoate played in St Helens 13-12 upset victory over [[Penrith Panthers|Penrith]] in the [[2023 World Club Challenge]].{{cite news |title=St Helens stun Penrith to win World Club Challenge |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/64666651 |work=BBC Sport}} On 21 September 2023, it was announced that Hopoate would be departing St Helens at the end of the 2023 Super League season.{{cite web|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/st-helens-confirm-11-departures-including-overseas-star-will-hopoate/|title=St Helens confirm 11 departures, including overseas star Will Hopoate|website=www.loverugbyleague.com}} Hopoate played 16 games for St Helens in the 2023 Super League season as the club finished third on the table. He played in St Helens narrow loss against the Catalans Dragons in the semi-final which ended St Helens four-year dominance of the competition.{{cite web|url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/23856044.paul-wellens-q-saints-review-year-look-2024/|title=Paul Wellens Q&A: Saints review of year and a look to 2024|website=www.sthelensstar.co.uk}} On 5 November 2023, immediately following the [[2023 Tonga tour of England]], he announced his retirement from professional rugby league.{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League |date=5 Nov 2023|title=Tonga veteran Will Hopoate announces retirement following conclusion of England test series: ‘What a ride it’s been’|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/tonga-veteran-will-hopoate-announces-retirement-following-conclusion-of-england-test-series-what-a-ride-its-been}} ==Representative career== ===NSW=== On 5 June 2011, Hopoate was selected to play [[Rugby League State of Origin|State of Origin]] for [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] in [[2011 State of Origin series|Game 2]] against [[Queensland rugby league team|Queensland]] at [[ANZ Stadium]]. He is the second youngest player behind [[Brad Fittler]] ever selected to play for NSW and he scored a try on debut helping NSW level the 3-game series at one all with an 18–8 win.{{cite web |url=https://www.perthnow.com.au/news/nsw/william-hopoate-scores-wonder-try-to-open-scoring-for-nsw-on-his-state-of-origin-debut-ng-63f1420f5c6d1db179e70f3a6eef8dfd |title=William Hopoate scores wonder try to open scoring for NSW on his State of Origin debut |publisher=PerthNow |date=16 June 2011 |access-date=11 December 2022}} ===Tonga=== In 2017, Hopoate elected to represent the nation of his ancestral heritage, [[Tonga national rugby league team|Tonga]]. He played fullback in every game of their stunning charge to the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup]] semi-finals. He was a tryscorer in their shock win over [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in the pool stage of the tournament.{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2017/11/11/tonga-stun-new-zealand-to-top-pool-b/|title = Tonga stun New Zealand to top Pool B|date = 11 November 2017}} He was named at fullback again for the historic first-ever Test match between Tonga and the [[Australian Kangaroos]] on 20 October 2018, played in front of a sold-out crowd at [[Mount Smart Stadium]], Auckland.{{Cite web|url=https://www.radionz.co.nz/news/sport/369091/kangaroos-withstand-spirited-tongan-challenge|title = Kangaroos withstand spirited Tongan challenge|date = 20 October 2018}} In October 2022, he was named in the [[2021 Men's Rugby League World Cup squads#Tonga|Tonga squad]] for the [[2021 Rugby League World Cup – Men's tournament|2021 Rugby League World Cup]].[https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/rugby-league-world-cup-2021-squad-lists/ Full list of every squad at the Rugby League World Cup 2021] ==Personal life== Outside of rugby league, Hopoate is an active member of [[the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints]]. As such Hopoate chose not to take part in his early league career and spent 2012 and 2013 serving a two-year [[Missionary|mission]] for The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church).{{cite news|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10725439&ref=rss|title=Young star leaving NRL for a mission for the church of Jesus of latter day saints|date=13 May 2011|agency=[[Newstalk ZB]]|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|access-date=3 December 2011}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== {{commons category}} *[https://www.bulldogs.com.au/teams/telstra-premiership/canterbury-bankstown-bulldogs/will-hopoate/ Canterbury Bulldogs profile] *[http://www.bulldogs.com.au/team/profiles/will_hopoate.html Bulldogs profile] *[http://www.rlwc2017.com/players?team=384#Will-Hopoate 2017 RLWC profile] {{St Helens -
